data.title: Energy, Metals, And Infrastructure: Where Wall Street Will Be 'Forced' To Invest Next
data.description:
Introduction

AI has become one of my most important topics. That's not because it's a hip theme to cover or because it produces some "cool" tech, but because I'm convinced that it's a truly transformative technology. That's why I called it "The Biggest Market Disruption Since The Industrial Revolution" in the title of an in-depth article on Sept. 6.

To me, this isn't just a transformative technology because of the way companies will adopt it, but also because of the potentially massive impact this can have on macroeconomic indicators like inflation, labor, company margins, GDP growth, and so many other factors.

So far, this has already been the case, as technology capex in 2025 as a percentage of the U.S. GDP has exceeded anything we have seen since at least the 1930s, as we can see below.

Moreover, as I wrote on X (that used to be Twitter) the other day, labor is already being disrupted. Despite solid GDP growth, we're seeing weakness in hiring (see below), which usually indicates elevated recession risks.

In that post, I noted that the old cycle has been broken. That cycle used to be that if GDP were growing, it would require higher capex from companies. As this capex was often spent on economic expansion, it would require people to operate. Now, that's different. If you allow me to paint with that broad brush of mine, we're now shifting to a cycle where capex is focused on computing power, which reduces operating expenses (higher margins). Companies are basically using capex to grow margins instead of growing their headcount.

These are just two major examples of why I believe it would be a huge mistake not to consistently remain on top of these critical developments. And that's putting it mildly.

So, after diving into the subject again in the past few days, this article is about a new development on Wall Street that I expect to have a major impact on sectors and the way "smart money" may deploy capital this year (and beyond).

To me, this isn't just a rotation but a reversal of a trade that has made investors a lot of money since 2009. It's all about code being "free" and atoms being expensive.

As that may sound vague, I would say, let's get right to it!

Have You Ever Heard of “Stranded Compute?”

"Compute" is a word I've used quite a bit in recent months. Every now and then, people ask me what that means. Basically, it refers to the computational power or the resources that are required to perform calculations. In the context that I use, it's the power that fuels AI.

Computing power is extremely important in the age of AI. That's why the largest hyperscalers are basically battling each other for AI leadership. The chart below shows this quite well, as the five biggest hyperscalers (just five companies!) are expected to spend more than $530 billion on capex this year. Next year, that number could be more than $600 billion.

I have to say that it's weird that we have gotten used to these big numbers. I know I sound repetitive, but think about this for a second. Five companies are expected to spend more than $600 billion on capex next year. As the chart also shows, that's roughly 30% of their revenues and will likely result in a free cash flow margin in the low-double-digit range.

This means at least two things:

  • These companies are basically in an arms race that requires them to throw as much money at AI, which turns them into capex-heavy companies. They are not the lean tech companies they used to be.
  • Despite this, their free cash flow is still expected to be positive, which shows just how strong these companies are. This is often forgotten, just how immense their financial power is.

On paper, all of this makes sense.

Unfortunately, there is a problem. That problem is power. While you can buy chips, the power that fuels these chips (data centers) is limited. As Elon Musk said on a recent Moonshots podcast with Peter Diamandis that I watched/listened to, he confirmed a challenge that I have discussed in many articles as well, which is that he expects a lot of stranded assets.

By that, he means that hyperscalers will have the newest chips, yet they lack the power to put these to work. It's like having the money to buy the fastest Porsches and Ferraris, yet all the surrounding gas stations are out of gas. Now, it doesn't matter how fast your cars are on paper.

As reported by Seeking Alpha, NVIDIA's (NVDA) Blackwell and Rubin chips are expected to see demand of more than $500 billion (in just two years!):

"We were surprised to see Vera Rubin details at CES and believe Rubin architecture will enhance/surpass Nvidia's AI platform appeal vs. Blackwell given increased architectural complexity with six new chips," said Citi analysts in a Wednesday investor note. "The company sees upside to prior $500B 2025/26 AI demand from both Blackwell and Rubin." - Seeking Alpha

Nvidia also sees 40% annual compounding data center capex growth between 2025 and 2030. Total AI infrastructure spending is expected to be $3 trillion to $4 trillion by 2030. These are simply blowout numbers by any means.

Moreover, Musk basically made the case that we are now at a point where billions worth of the best GPUs will be idle because local utility companies cannot get permits for substations or find enough transformers (among many other challenges in the power space).

This means two things:

  • The market has priced in the semiconductor bull case for years. That's why Nvidia and its peers have gone through the roof. I think we all know what the stock prices of companies like Nvidia and Broadcom (AVGO) look like. However, as we can see below (FactSet data), that's justified, as AVGO is expected to grow its EPS by 47% this year, followed by 41% and 26% in the next two years. Nonetheless, this isn't a "hidden" thesis. I'm convinced this is the most obvious part of the AI thesis.

  • The power and infrastructure thesis isn't as "hidden" as it was in prior years, yet I believe it will be shoved in everyone's faces this year due to the bottleneck challenges that are now arising. Basically, that thesis is going from a theory on paper to a reality this year. At least, I think that's a fair way of describing it.

I believe that the evidence for this thesis is getting strong. This includes energy stocks that produce the natural gas needed to power data centers. Below, we're looking at the total return ratio between energy (XLE) and the tech-heavy QQQ ETF (QQQ). A rising line means that energy stocks are outperforming. A declining line means the opposite.

Since AI went mainstream, this ratio has been in a downtrend, fueled by a decline in oil prices. However, in recent months, the ratio has been bottoming. I think we'll see an uptrend in the quarters (and years) ahead. That does not mean that big tech is toast. It only means that there could be a lot more alpha in the next wave of AI beneficiaries, like energy.

This brings me to the next part of the article (and my thesis).

Could This Be the End of Software Dominance?

For more than a decade, software companies have been leading the market.

The chart below shows the ratio between software companies (IGV) and the S&P 500. Basically, since 2007, software has been the place to be. That ended in 2021. In 2023, software started to weaken again relative to the market (the ratio declined).

I haven't owned a single software company in my dividend portfolio since I started it in 2021. Initially, I missed out, as software, in general, is genius. It's easy to scale, asset-light, and supported by companies that need to innovate and streamline their operations.

However, I don't regret not buying software for a second. For starters, software has lost its shine in recent years, as the ratio above shows. That means, unless I were to pick truly exceptional software companies, I likely didn't miss out.

However, that's not even my main point here.

My main point is the impact of AI on software.

Before AI became a thing, software companies had wider moats, as writing code wasn't easy. It took large teams and a lot of effort to create new software and to market it. AI is making it much cheaper and faster to produce software. This is from an article written in August 2025:

In a recent conversation with Meta's Mark Zuckerberg, Satya Nadella, CEO at Microsoft, revealed that some of the company's coding projects are now exclusively "written by software." The chief executive explained that 20-30% of the code in Microsoft's repositories is now AI-generated.

This is a stark reminder of the pace at which AI tools are being adopted within the IT industry. For more evidence, look to Stack Overflow's 2024 Developer Survey, which found the number of developers using AI tools has increased from 44% to 62% in the last 12 months alone. - ITPro

Depending on when these numbers were observed, we're now roughly a year further. When adding the accelerating speed of AI innovation, I think we can make the case that the price of code is rapidly dropping.

This does not mean that all software companies will go out of business. Some will be able to adopt AI very effectively and use it to their benefit. Others may go out of business, while new successful software companies may be founded because of AI.

My point is that, generally speaking, competitive benefits in software (their moats) are being eroded. This is already hurting the valuations of successful software players. Adobe (ADBE), for example, trades at just 15.7x earnings (see below), despite expectations of 12% and 13% EPS growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively. Its long-term P/E average is slightly more than 31.0x. I'm not saying that 15.7x EPS is justified or that ADBE will go out of business, but I am saying that this is one of the re-ratings likely caused by eroding moats in the industry.

Allow me to repeat this without being too repetitive. What I'm saying is that AI risks are causing massive valuation re-rating risks. For investors, that's the biggest risk, as it means that the competitive landscape has changed. That obviously needs to be reflected in valuations.

This is bullish for three big sectors.

Wall Street's Trifecta?

To reiterate my previous statement, I'm not saying that software is dead. My thesis is that we're getting a market re-rating, which will make finding alpha in this space much harder.

I think capital is flowing into at least three other areas.

For starters, I think capital will move into the grid and power generation stocks. The grid is old, and in an environment where a single data center can consume as much power as a small town, or even a big town, I think we are looking at decades of grid and infrastructure modernization.

For this, we need grid equipment players like GE Vernova (GEV), which produces natural gas turbines; Eaton (ETN), which produces a wide range of products and services for data centers and grid modernization; and Quanta Services (PWR), which is an engineering company supporting utilities, midstream companies, renewable players, and many more.

Moreover, we need to be aware that the fastest way to add power is by better utilizing the power that's available. That's where Tesla (TSLA) comes in. In general, I think it's a perfect "new economy" stock due to its exposure to robotics, autonomous driving, and power storage. Unfortunately, I have a hard time dealing with its valuation. If everything goes right for Tesla, it has exposure to power storage, cars that can both collect data and drive autonomously using AI, robots to benefit from physical AI, and the factories to actually produce these.

Smaller players in this space that I like include Generac (GNRC), which produces generators of all sizes. I believe these companies will be increasingly important as well.

That brings me to the second group.

Power suppliers will be key. In this group, I put oil and gas companies as well as independent power producers like Vistra Corp. (VST) and Constellation Energy (CEG), which own the largest nuclear power fleets in the U.S. Just recently, Vistra closed a deal with Meta Platforms (META). I think this is just the start. Also, Vistra operates in Texas, a market with a lot of pricing power and data center demand (see below).

Moreover, as I mentioned in the article already, I see strong tailwinds for oil and gas producers. These are the companies that will produce the commodities that go into power plants. Moreover, some of these will benefit even more, as they have what it takes to turn their own commodities into power. Chevron (CVX) is one of these giants.

Chevron is building a natural gas plant in West Texas with a capacity of up to 5 GW. That's genius for a number of reasons:

  • West Texas is a booming data center market due to loose regulations, plenty of land, cooling water access, and cheap energy. That's why I invest in Texas Pacific Land (TPL) and LandBridge (LB). Both are currently working with hyperscalers (neither has an official data center announcement yet).
  • As I have explained in some energy articles, because the Permian Basin (in West Texas and New Mexico) produces so much natural gas with limited pipeline capacity, it's the cheapest place for natural gas in the U.S. Many producers even have to pay others to get rid of natural gas as a by-product. Chevron can now turn this into power.

In general, I'm bullish on energy, as I discuss in this article. However, especially in light of the AI boom and what are now becoming obvious limitations, I see no way around it.

Moreover, renewables also have a place in this mix. If we see a bigger buildout of power storage, renewable energy can start to fill bigger gaps. In this area, I like some solar stocks like First Solar (FSLR). Although I prefer natural gas, to me, it seems there's a place for these companies in the power mix (again, assuming a bigger power storage buildout).

Last but not least, I think there's a case to be made for metals as well.

What do data centers and the grid both need? The answer is copper.

Here's what S&P Global had to say:

S&P Global's comprehensive study identifies a transformative trajectory for copper demand, projecting a surge from 28 million metric tons in 2025 to 42 million metric tons by 2040 – a 50% increase that underscores the metal's pivotal role in multiple technological and economic domains. However, meeting the call on copper confronts significant supply obstacles both above and below ground. The study projects a potential 10 million metric ton copper shortfall by 2040 without meaningful supply expansion. This demand growth – and addressing the looming challenges to meeting it – is what this report is about. - S&P Global

The best thing is that copper isn't a major cost of a data center. For example, if large data center projects cost billions of dollars, it does not matter much to them if copper is trading at $3, $7, or $10. Sure, there's a difference, but that's not the driving force of prices.

This adds price inelasticity of the biggest demand drivers to a mix of tailwinds that include limited supply growth. That's why I think copper has more room to rise, especially if we get a broader-based economic recovery.

Here, I like Freeport-McMoRan (FCX) and Southern Copper (SCCO). However, I also like more diversified players like BHP (BHP) and even companies focused on more niche metals like Teck Resources (TECK).

That's where I think the alpha will be.

It also explains why I think the software outperformance has faded and will make room for "hard asset" outperformance in the years (maybe >10 years) ahead.

I'm positioned for it.

Takeaway

"Rotation" has become one of my favorite words in recent months.

It applies to the market's biggest drivers as well. After more than a decade of software leadership and a few years where the semiconductor thesis has become increasingly obvious, I think we're now looking at a new trend due to the realization that "code is free" (read: the marginal cost of code is declining) and atoms to power the whole thing are increasingly hard to get.

That's great for companies operating in these bottlenecks, making them the likely leaders of the next rotation.

That's why I'm investing in energy, infrastructure, and other wide-moat companies that I expect to generate substantial alpha in 2026 and beyond.

Needless to say, I'll provide you with more food for thought and continue to update the situation.

Risks to My Thesis

The biggest risk is an economic downturn. It could hurt commodity prices and cause delays in capex projects. Moreover, innovation in energy efficiency could hurt demand, although I believe we're at a stage where any efficiency gains are being offset by demand.

Also, all companies mentioned have their own company risks. Do your own research before putting a dollar to work in any of these.

data.title: OMS Energy: A Management-Owned Company The Market Has Yet To Recognize
data.description:
Thesis

OMS Energy Technologies (OMSE) looks like an undiscovered opportunity in the energy sector. It is one of those rare situations where a company's fundamentals and its valuation seem to live in completely different universes. At first glance, OMS looks like a struggling small-cap: recently IPO'd, little analyst coverage, a tiny float, heavy customer concentration, and declining revenue. But beneath the surface lies something very different: a debt-free, high-margin, and cash-loaded specialty solutions provider with longstanding relationships with global oil companies, expanding international demand, and a decade-long CEO who personally bought the business during hardship out of a conglomerate and turned it into a cash machine.

At the current price, OMS trades at roughly 1.5 times enterprise value to operating cash flow, one of the lowest multiples I've ever seen in the market. The company has $126 million of cash on the balance sheet, which is more than half its market capitalization, and consistently generates 20%+ operating margins with strong free cash flow. It is rare to find a business with this level of profitability, insider ownership, and balance sheet strength trading at distressed valuations. To me, this disconnect looks like a case of the market judging the book by its cover, and the real story appears only once you turn a few pages.

In this article, I will walk through OMS' unusual history, analyze the business model, look into the Aramco relationship, and break down why I believe the market is dramatically mispricing this stock. Given how unusually low the current valuation is, even a slight normalization towards industry multiples would imply multiple hundred percent upside over the coming years, with the balance sheet providing robust downside protection. Therefore, I rate the stock a strong buy.

Business Model

Founded in 1972, OMS manufactures components such as the following that are critical in oil and gas well construction: surface wellhead systems, Christmas trees, specialty connectors, high-strength pipes, and premium threading services. These systems are installed where the wellbore reaches the surface, bridging the underground formations and the equipment that controls production at the surface. In particular, OMS supplies the hardware that keeps the well sealed, stable, and safe as operators drill thousands of feet into the earth under high-pressure and corrosive conditions.

OMS does not provide typical commodity steel products, instead specializing in high-precision components that directly impact well integrity, pressure containment, and operational safety. In upstream operations, reliability is critical, as a single failure can cost millions of dollars in lost operational hours, remediation, and reduced output. This technical specialization gives OMS a reliable degree of pricing stability and helps create long-term customer relationships, particularly with national oil companies that require suppliers to be capable of meeting strict operational standards.

Importantly, OMS is headquartered in Singapore, while their primary manufacturing and finishing operations are located in Southeast Asia, and this proximity of customers to their operational facilities provides the advantages of logistical accessibility and access to customers. These are within short logistical reach of these facilities, giving OMS cost advantages and direct access to its customers.

Company History

OMS has a relatively atypical origin story, as outlined in a recent interview with CEO How Meng Hock (age 57), as he explained the company's history and evolution to its current form. The company has existed in various forms since it was founded in 1972. As recently as 2010, OMS was a division inside Sumitomo Corporation, one of Japan's largest conglomerates and trading firms. How Meng Hock joined Sumitomo in 2012 and began running the OMS organization in 2014. Soon afterward, the U.S. shale revolution caused a collapse in global oilfield service pricing. Much like most other component suppliers, OMS was hit hard. As demand weakened and profitability came under pressure, Sumitomo - not being an industrial operator - became less willing to support a division that was both struggling and outside its primary operating sectors, and so OMS seemed destined for either divestment or closure.

At that point, How Meng Hock approached Sumitomo with a bold proposal: rather than folding the company or selling to an external buyer, he would acquire it himself through a management buyout (MBO). Negotiations began in 2019 but were slowed by the Covid pandemic. Discussions concluded in June 2023 after both parties had reached an agreement.

Sumitomo wrote down OMS's goodwill and assets in their entirety, which left the division with a negative equity value. The excess cash was extracted, and all outstanding loans were removed from the balance sheet. Next, How Meng Hock acquired OMS for $2 million, and Sumitomo provided an $8.8 million loan with a three-year maturity period, which supported ongoing working capital requirements as the business continued operating independently.

OMS had fully repaid the $8.8 million loan within 8 months of the agreement being signed, which was over two years ahead of schedule. Once freed from its additional financial obligations and corporate constraints, the underlying business quickly demonstrated its ability to operate as a lean, cash-generating company with a loyal customer base and proven engineering capabilities.

Today, OMS is debt-free and holds $126 million in cash, of which $33 million are proceeds from their IPO. As part of the listing process, the company completed a required valuation review in March 2024. Despite having been written down to zero a year earlier, OMS' value was recorded at $51 million, reflecting the real economic value of its assets, production facilities, technical approvals, and long-term customer contracts.

In May 2025, OMS went public on the NASDAQ at $9.00 per share. The CEO kept 62% of the outstanding stock, putting him in a position as a controlling shareholder. His personal financial situation is now tied directly to the value he generates from both his role as an operator and an allocator of capital.

A valid question to ask here is why OMS would go public if the company was already generating plenty of profit? According to the CEO, the motivation was strategic rather than financial. NASDAQ listings offer greater transparency, credibility, and a registered governance framework that makes it easier to build trust with major national oil companies, global energy players, and banks - all of whom are critical for OMS' long-term expansion, including potential M&A.

The Saudi Aramco Relationship: Risk or Strategic Asset?

A commonly (and rightfully) cited concern around OMS emerges from the fact that in the 2025 fiscal year, Saudi Aramco represented 67% of the company's revenue. Such a high degree of concentration naturally raises concerns and represents a risk investors need to take seriously. The primary risk here is clear: if Saudi Aramco were to remove OMS from its supplier roster, the company would experience a severe reduction of revenue.

The higher revenue levels OMS experienced in FY2024 and FY2025 were partially driven by an unusually high number of orders from Saudi Aramco. In H1 FY2026, call-off orders returned to more typical levels, which led to a decrease in year-on-year revenue. Nonetheless, the H1 FY2026 report shows that OMS remained highly profitable in spite of fewer call-offs, as they posted gross margins above 28% and operating margins above 21%, which should give reassurance to potential investors. Although, if Saudi Aramco were to drop OMS as a supplier and 67% of revenue were suddenly removed, profitability would likely fall sharply, with long-term difficulties exacerbated by fixed costs, underutilized production capacity, and operational leverage.

However, a closer look at the nature of the relationship provides important context. OMS is a technically qualified supplier of engineered wellhead components to Aramco, a status that requires multiyear certification processes, strict audits, and a demonstrable history of delivering quality products. This is not a supplier chosen by Aramco solely on price - it is the result of well-established technical alignment. In January 2024, OMS and Aramco entered into a 10-year Corporate Purchase Agreement. While the agreement contains no minimum purchase volumes, the CEO estimates the contract volume at $120-200 million annually, as well as formalizing OMS as an approved supplier for Saudi Aramco, which is an achievement that should not be understated.

Furthermore, the first production site in Saudi Arabia was built in 2008, and then in 2021, OMS moved to a new production facility that had more than twice the space and employed locals from Saudi Arabia. Combined with the 10-year corporate purchase agreement, it looks to me like this relationship is deepening, which seriously mitigates the risk of severe revenue loss from Saudi Aramco.

In this context it is worth highlighting that OMS is also actively widening its customer base. Recent client acquisitions in Pakistan, Angola, Indonesia, and Thailand are early indicators of success in the CEO's expansion strategy. He has stated explicitly that diversifying revenue is a priority, with the understanding that diversifying their portfolio will take time given the size of Aramco relative to other regional operators.

Excessive customer concentration at this level is a genuine risk, but it exists within the context of a long-term, mutually beneficial relationship. Further clarity from management would be helpful in fully understanding the underlying dynamics.

Financials

Assessing OMS requires extra context and nuance because the company only went public recently and therefore has a limited subset of disclosed financial periods. OMS follows a fiscal year running from April to March, which means FY2024 spans from April 2023 to March 2024, FY2025 spans from April 2024 to March 2025, and the latest H1 FY2026 covers April-September 2025.

Despite the relatively short public history, the latest available numbers present OMS as a firm that is a highly profitable business with comparatively stable margins across varying levels of Saudi Aramco call-off activity.

Revenue increased by $23 million, from $181 million in FY2024 to $204 million in FY2025, partially as a result of an uptick in call-offs under the Aramco long-term purchase agreement. FY2025 concluded with a similar growth in profitability. Net profit increased from $35 million in FY2024 to $47 million in FY2025, reflecting not only higher volumes of operational output but also greater operating leverage and rigorous liquidity management.

A similar story is seen in the cash flow records. Operating cash flow came in at $21 million in FY2024, then by FY2025 had nearly doubled to $41 million; a notable reason for this is OMS' low capex requirements, which lead to converting a large portion of earnings into cash.

During the most recent reporting period, H1 2026, we get a view of OMS operating under more typical conditions, following the elevated Aramco call-offs of the prior year; revenue for the six months was $82.8 million, and net profit was $14.6 million. Profitability remained solid despite the reduced volume of call-offs. Gross margins balanced out at 28.2%, and operating margins were 21.6%. OMS also generated $26.4 million in operating cash flow during the first half of FY2026, also as a result of inventory being converted to cash as Aramco orders were fulfilled. Even under normalized call-off levels, the company continues to produce robust cash from operations.

Valuation and Upside Scenarios

Valuing OMS calls for a greater depth of analysis than simply reviewing headline numbers, as its basic valuation indicators are distorted by its unusually large cash balance. With a market capitalization of $201 million and a net cash position of $126 million - as of 30th September 2025 - the enterprise value of OMS is just $75 million.

The valuation of OMS on an earnings basis alone already seems compelling. Based on data from FY2025, OMS earned $47 million in net profit, resulting in a P/E ratio of 4.3×. Based upon the most recently available data, from H1 FY2026, recording a net profit of $14.6 million means OMS would have a P/E of 7x if one extrapolates annual profits as $29.2 million ($14.6 million x 2). For a company that is debt-free, demonstrates strong margins, and operates predominantly amid long-term customer relationships, these valuations already stand out as conservative.

Because OMS holds substantial cash and carries no debt, I would argue that enterprise value (EV), which nets out the cash position, is the more informative metric for valuation. With an EV of $75 million, the valuation on earnings becomes even more compelling. Based on FY2025 profit, OMS trades at just 1.7× EV/Earnings, and even on the annualized H1 FY2026 earnings, the multiple is only 2.7×.

A similarly interesting narrative can be read in the development of cash flows. OMS generated $21 million in operating cash flow (OCF) in FY2024 and $41 million in FY2025. Continuing the trend of growth, in H1 FY2026 the operating cash flow came in at $26.3 million, which annualized would total $52.6 million. On that basis, the stock trades at only 3.9× Price/OCF, while EV/OCF is just 1.5×. Investors would usually associate these metrics with a company under real financial stress, which further states how low OMS' valuation currently is.

To remain prudent, it would be reasonable to adjust H1 FY2026 cash flow downward, as the period's figures benefited from an inventory unwinding of $12.2 million as the company fulfilled Aramco orders from the prior year. Without the aforementioned cash injection, an adjusted OCF would be $14.1 million for the half-year, or $28.2 million annualized. Even operating under this overly conservative assumption, the business still trades at 7.2× Price/OCF and only 2.8× EV/OCF, which would still firmly place OMS in undervalued territory.

With such a large range of caveats for OMS' valuation over the previous few years, it is difficult to calculate this accurately and consistently. Nonetheless, if you compare their currently quoted EV/EBIT of just 2.3×, on a TTM basis, with the industry average of 13.7×, it is clear there is a disconnect between the current valuation and real value of OMS based upon the evidence presented so far. Even a modest re-rating to a 7× multiple, which is still conservative among peers, would imply at least a 200% upside from current levels.

Management has not issued formal forward guidance, so investors cannot currently take any inspiration on company trajectory from OMS and so must rely on the financial history currently available. However, all the valuation methods examined above provide a consistent picture of a firm that is valued similarly to one in a distressed situation, while in reality they have presented themselves as resilient and profitable.

Risks

Finally, a balanced assessment also has to acknowledge the key risks present. Whilst the previously examined issue of customer concentration seems the most imminent risk, there are others that should also be explored.

One point of concern is a result of OMS' unusually large cash balance. With $126 million in cash and no debt, the company might have a lot of financial flexibility, but this also increases the likelihood of misallocating capital, which could have immediate and profound ramifications. Management has made it clear that acquisitions will play a key role in the long-term growth strategy. Choosing an incompatible firm, overpaying, or failing to integrate the new entity effectively could destroy shareholder value. Therefore, investors will need to watch closely how OMS deploys its cash.

The company's ownership structure also has potential downsides. Due to the CEO's controlling share of 62% of equity, he has a dominant vote in major corporate decisions. While this alignment can be positive for long-term holders, it relegates the authority of minority shareholders, who will be unable to effectively challenge How Meng Hock across many key strategic areas such as capital allocation, acquisition targets, or capital returns.

Macro conditions introduce another layer of uncertainty within the upstream oil and gas sector, where company values are tied to commodity prices. Adverse market conditions, such as those we have seen after the U.S. shale revolution, could reduce drilling activity, slow capex cycles, and weaken demand for wellhead components. National oil companies are more stable than private operators, but they also adjust their procurement cycles when prices weaken.

Competitive forces within the industry must also be taken into account, which also contains a number of large and well-capitalized global players such as SLB (SLB), Halliburton (HAL), and Baker Hughes (BKR). While OMS' USP can be found in their specialism in focused product niches where certification and technical fit matter more than scale, larger firms could still pursue the same high-margin categories and pressure OMS through lower pricing. Therefore, maintaining a technical differentiation and retaining their reputation for high-quality service will be critical for OMS to defend its margins in the future.

Whilst these risks do not undermine the investment thesis, they are an important reminder of factors that investors should always consider: customer concentration, capital allocation risks, a majority shareholder, and a competitive operating environment.

Bottom Line

OMS Energy Technology presents an opportunity that is not seen frequently in the small-cap universe: high margins, strong returns on capital, and robust cash generation, while continuing to trade at an exceptionally low valuation. Additionally, the company is debt-free, holds $126 million in cash as part of their strategy, and continues to produce operating margins above 20% amidst a return to more standard rates of call-offs. When you combine this with an experienced CEO who is financially invested in the company's success, this appears to be an attractive opportunity for investors seeking quality at a discounted price.

Currently, OMS trades at just 2.7× EV/Earnings based on annualized H1 FY2026 earnings and only around 1.5× EV to annualized H1 FY2026 operating cash flow.

The market is pricing OMS as though one or more of the key risks will inevitably materialize, effectively valuing the company as a struggling asset. In such a scenario, if one of the risks were to play out, the current low valuation limits the remaining downside. But if nothing adverse occurs, I do not expect this valuation disconnect to persist. As the company delivers steady profitability, deploys its cash prudently, and broadens its customer base, a re-rating towards more conventional industry multiples becomes increasingly likely.

That said, genuine left-tail risks do exist, and I reflect that by keeping the position size modest within my portfolio. Still, for investors who can tolerate small-cap volatility, OMS offers an interesting asymmetric opportunity. I currently rate the stock a Strong Buy, with the potential for 100-300% upside over a multi-year time frame as the valuation aligns more closely with similar market operators.

data.title: Synchrony Financial: The Dip Made Valuation Synchronized With Fundamentals
data.description:
It has been nearly seven months since my previous coverage of Synchrony Financial (SYF), which has already risen by 40%. Needless to say, my hold rating was a bad call. As I revisit this stock, I have some things I wish I realized sooner. Its fundamentals are sound and intact, supported by its strong domestic coverage and strategic partnerships. Last January 12, the stock fell in lieu of President Trump’s call for setting a 10% cap on credit card interest rates. While I believe that the price drop was valid, this could open new entry opportunities for investors. Technicals adhere to it as the selloff is leading to overselling. Nonetheless, caution is still essential.

Synchrony Financial: How It Has Been Recently

As a consumer finance business, Synchrony Financial is highly exposed to macroeconomic volatility. It is also cyclical and highly sensitive to inflation and interest rates. In the past five quarters, we have seen how the policy easing cycle affected its performance.

In Q3 2025, its interest income amounted to $5.7B, down by 0.9% YoY from $5.8B. Lower interest rates lowered the yields on consumer loans and credit cards. After all, these are typically variable-rate loans, so changes made by the Fed can easily affect them.

On a lighter note, it appears that SYF has already adjusted to the rate cut cycle. As you can see, the interest income improved by 0.95% from my previous coverage. It also attracted borrowers in the past two quarters. More interestingly, this helped improve SYF’s credit quality. In fact, delinquent loans over 30 and 90 days dropped from the previous year.

Lastly, it benefited from the rate cut cycle as interest expense dropped as well. This was most evident in interest on deposits, which fell by over $150M, or 16%. As a result, net interest income on a YoY and QoQ basis.

My Thoughts on Trump’s 10% Capping: Pluses and Minuses

A few days ago, Trump’s call for a 10% credit card rate cap made noise in the stock market. Consumer finance stocks like SYF fell by over 10% in just a day. Well, the market reaction is valid due to their cautious stance.

Now, why does it matter? SYF credit card and consumer loan rates vary per type. Often, rates begin with introductory offers before moving to variable rates tied to the prime rate. SYF’s APR typically ranges from the high teens to the low 30s. It can even go as high as 39.99% in case of penalties. So, imposing a maximum of 10% will reduce the interest income and profitability of SYF.

In addition, SYF and peers may tighten their lending to high-risk borrowers. This move may also lower credit access among borrowers. This should not be surprising since credit cards and consumer loans are typically not secured and have variable rates.

On the brighter side, lower credit card rates can support consumption and boost usage among credit card holders. Lower credit card rates mean lower borrowing costs. Low-risk borrowers and the loyal customer base of SYF may benefit from it. SYF may benefit from it since higher credit card usage and demand can increase its loan base. In addition, more borrowers may repay on time, which can improve credit quality.

Will SYF Survive if It is Approved?

Before I answer this question, I’d like to explain that the likelihood of it getting approved is still low. So, going below $70 at this point may be an overreaction. Anyway, Trump alone cannot implement this since it requires the approval of Congress, which can be a big obstacle. Legislation from the US House and Senate is needed before it becomes ready for signing into law. Even Trump’s supporters are divided here. Lastly, we must understand that there are still legal and practical barriers to pass through. Again, there is no law allowing it yet. Also, industry groups may gear up to challenge it. We should also know that banking and other business groups will oppose this idea since it will hurt lenders and even consumers.

Going back to the main topic, I believe that SYF can survive this. SYF is still liquid since it has $19.0B in cash and debt securities, which can cover delinquencies. Also, I believe the 10% rate cap will only apply to the incoming or new credit card balances once it becomes law. Existing balances may remain under the original conditions.

Meanwhile, it has a high loan-to-deposit ratio. But note that only $2.8B, or 3.5% of the total deposits, are demand deposits. So, deposit-run risk is low. Its cash and debt securities can easily cover the whole amount. Their difference is still more than enough to cover borrowings. This is also less of a concern since only 24% of the total will mature within a year. Also, 33% of the total amount has a maturity of three years and longer. Hence, it still has enough capital buffer to sustain its operations and dividends.

Lastly, SYF has a significant domestic market presence, allowing it to maintain a solid customer base. This can be amplified by its partnership with PayPal (PYPL), which is one of the leading e-wallets, online money transfers, and payment methods across the globe. PYPL partners with SYF to issue PYPL-branded credit products to consumers, like the PayPal Credit financing line and the PayPal Cashback Mastercard.

Valuation

In my previous coverage, I noted the fundamental strength of the company but hesitated about its valuation. Using various price ratios like P/E, P/B, and P/S, I concluded that SYF was overvalued then. However, I also failed to note that a higher multiple than the five-year average does not automatically mean overvaluation. It just means that the stock is just more expensive than in previous years. Price ratios just reveal their relative value, not the intrinsic value.

As I updated its valuation, price ratios still said the same thing. Yet, the market does not always care much about it. Why? The price will most likely increase as long as financial metrics like the EPS, revenue, and book value increase.

To prove it, we can use the regression analysis. The Multiple R of 0.785 means a correction of 79%. This means that if sales increase, there is a 79% probability that the price will follow its trend. This is proven by the P-value of 0.7%, which means that less than 1% of their relationship happened by chance. To avoid confusion, sales or revenue per share is the sum of net interest income and non-interest income divided by the number of shares. To make it simple, I used the revenue or sales per share on Seeking Alpha. The same applies to the EPS, which shows a higher correlation with the price and a lower P-value. Hence, the statistical analysis justifies the recent price uptrend. The dip just also opened a new entry point.

Technicals

SYF’s technicals are still bullish despite the 10% stock price plunge. The price still coincides with the 50-day SMA, so the possibility of a rebound is still there. The price and the 50-day SMA are still above the 200-day SMA, which means that current momentum is still stronger than the average in the past trading year. Also, the upward trend of the 50-day SMA shows that market sentiments are still generally optimistic.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show some interesting changes. The MACD line is already trending downwards below the signal line due to the increasing selling volume. The histogram is now within negative territory, which implies that selling volume has already outpaced buying volume. However, the selloff may open new buying opportunities. Its RSI shows that the stock is nearly oversold, which can entice new entries and/or re-entries. Also, the recent dip was quite justified. This was not solely related to credit card rate woes. The stock became overbought before the news broke out. At its price, entering may be ideal.

Conclusion

Synchrony Financial may experience some weaknesses related to the potential credit card rate capping. But its solid customer base, robust liquidity, high credit quality, and strategic partnership with PYPL may shield its operations. Valuation is more expensive than in previous years but reasonable. Technicals remain strong even after a dip because this may lead to overselling and open new buying opportunities. I upgrade my rating from hold to buy.

data.title: Robotics News At CES All About Platforms
data.description:
According to most predictions, this year was going to be the breakout year for robotics at CES. And, in fact, that was the case. But while the physical products made the biggest initial splash at the show, it’s the news about robotics platforms and tools that will have the most long-term impact.

Sure, there was a huge number of robotic devices and demonstrations available to see in Las Vegas, but most all of them were not ready for primetime and certainly aren’t going to be commercial products anytime soon. The innovations introduced by chip makers, system builders, IP-focused companies and more, however, will start to make a meaningful difference starting this year. Architectures, reference platforms, software tools and related sub-components built and debuted by companies like Nvidia (NVDA), AMD, Intel (INTC), Qualcomm (QCOM), Arm (ARM), and others are going to enable the coming Physical AI revolution to get off the ground.

Qualcomm kicked things off with the latest edition to its robotics platform, the Dragonwing IQ10 SOC (System on Chip). Designed for the most advanced robotics applications, including the “brains” for humanoid robots, the IQ10 brings together many of the company’s most advanced component technologies. It incorporates up to 18 compute-grade Oryon CPU cores, an Adreno GPU, Hexagon NPU, Spectra ISP (Image Signal Processor), support for up to 20 cameras and more, with a total system-wide TOPS performance number of 700 according to Qualcomm. The IQ10 also incorporates a real-time safety subsystem to help enable support for important functional safety standards, which are going to be extremely critical for most robotics applications.

In addition to the new chip, the company also touted its range of other Dragonwing industrial processors and software tools, noting that the company’s recent acquisition of Arduino allows it to reach an extremely large range of potential robotics developers - from students and enthusiasts to industrial professionals - with a consistent tool chain.

At the Nvidia keynote, CEO Jensen Huang described the entire robotics platform that his company is working to create. From upgraded versions of its Jetson and Thor compute platforms - the new Jetson T4000 is powered by Nvidia’s latest Blackwell GPU architecture - to a whole range of models and training tools, Nvidia laid out an impressive vision for how it’s moving the robotics market forward. What was most noticeable is how much of the company’s efforts in robotics are now focused on software and AI models. The new Cosmos Transfer and Cosmos Predict 2.5 models are the latest iterations of its world models, designed for simulations and training of robots and autonomous machines in real-world environments. Cosmos Reason 2 is Nvidia’s newest VLM (Vision Language Model) and Isaac GROOT N1.6 is a VLA (Vision Language Action) model - arguably the LLM equivalents of the robotics world. They’re both intended to let robots see, understand, and react to objects and actions in the real world, with GROOT specifically focused on full body control for humanoid robots.

Intel’s keynote also made a brief mention of the robotics-related efforts the company is making with an Edge AI-focused version of its new Core Ultra Series 3 (codenamed “Panther Lake”) platform. Though many people don’t immediately think of Intel when it comes to robotics, the company has had a big presence in the industrial robotics market for a very long time. Now, with the dramatically improved GPU capabilities in Core Ultra Series 3 - as well as an enhanced NPU - Intel clearly sees an opportunity to tap into the excitement around physical AI and robotics. Intel’s Robotics AI Suite was recently updated to support Core Ultra Series 3, and the company introduced an industrial-grade Edge AI version of Panther Lake at CES. This marks the first time Intel has done a simultaneous launch of mainstream PC and edge-focused chips, emphasizing its desire to quickly jump into robotics and other Edge AI applications.

At the AMD keynote, CEO Dr. Lisa Su also made a big point to emphasize AMD’s interest in robotics as part of a broad sweeping narrative about how the company’s reach has grown. While there was no discussion about specific robotics-related products, the company did bring an impressive-looking humanoid robot called GENE.01 developed by Italian firm Generative Bionics (recently spun out from the Italian Institute of Technology (IIT)) to the stage that was touted to be powered by AMD technology. Specifically, the robot leverages AMD’s FPGAs for embedded vision and sensor response, as well as AMD CPUs, GPUs and its open-source ROCm software stack. Initially promised by the end of this year, the GENE.01 robot incorporates a number of intriguing innovations, including whole-body tactile sensors - connected to the FPGAs - enabling a touch-sensitive human “skin” to the robot, which is supposed to allow for safer interactions with humans.

The fact that the robot didn’t walk onto or off the stage was a bit disappointing - and suggests that the initial timeline could be a bit overeager. In a separate meeting I had with Generative Bionics CEO Daniele Pucci, however, I learned more about the company’s heritage from IIT and its 20+-year history of building robots that perform a great deal of very precise movements. More importantly, I also started to really appreciate their partnership with AMD and how the experience of working with a company with that kind of robotics history could have a very positive long-term impact on AMD’s future robotic initiatives.

Semiconductor design leader Arm did not make any specific robotics-related product announcements at CES, but it did reveal that the company has recently done a reorganization that pulls together the automotive, IoT, and robotics efforts into a single group, led by industry veteran Drew Henry. In my conversations with Drew, he emphasized Arm’s very long history in robotics, as well as real-time systems, functional safety, microcontrollers, software systems linking all these pieces, and many other elements that are an essential part of building robotics platforms. Needless to say, I’m expecting to hear a lot more about robotics-related initiatives from Arm over the next few years.

What also came up during that discussion was something that I heard from other suppliers as well. Much of the work that many of them have done in the automotive market - particularly with regard to autonomous driving - has obvious relevance and application in robotics. Nvidia’s Jensen Huang has been talking about ADAS as a form of Physical AI for some time, so no surprise there. However, it also became clear that just as smartphone-related technologies can impact PCs (and vice versa), there are significant differences in the expectations and demands around robotics versus autonomous driving. Hence, just finding success in one market does not ensure success in the other. As always, it boils down to having the right combination of hardware, software, platforms, architectures, and other elements in order to create products that the market is willing to accept.

Of course, with robotics, there are still other factors to consider. First, as mentioned earlier, one thing that became abundantly clear at this year’s CES is that the full, widespread commercialization of humanoid-style robots is still many years away - and initial price points are likely to be extremely high. But even beyond these technical and economic concerns, another issue is social acceptance of these devices. Unlike most any other technology product category we’ve ever seen, I think humanoid-style robots are going to face a psychological barrier to adoption that goes well beyond even some of the concerns that have been raised regarding autonomous cars.

Robots in science fiction is one thing, but in everyday real life? Well, that’s another. (And it’s not just me. In numerous conversations around the show, I heard similar sentiments.) Plus, there are enormous physical safety issues around multi-hundred-pound metal devices traipsing through our home and work environments. Personally, I think incremental steps between a robotic vacuum cleaner and real-life Rosie the Robot are going to be essential, but it will be fascinating to see how this market evolves.

Even with these concerns, however, it’s clear that the excitement and anticipation for a robust robotics market is going to drive many important tech innovations over the next few years. It’s also going to create a huge range of opportunities for existing players as well as for startups and other as-yet-unknown companies. As with most things, people will eventually start to get used to robotics devices, so the question won’t be if the robotics market happens, but when.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why UiPath Is Poised To Outperform In The Age Of AI And Automation
data.description:
UiPath (NYSE: PATH) has done well since I recommended a buy in my March 7, 2025, article on the company. It has increased by 53.59% since then, as of January 7, 2026. In comparison, the S&P 500 (SPX) has risen by 20.87%.

One of the biggest reasons UiPath is outperforming the market is that it is one of the few companies helping its enterprise clients solve a problem that has persisted since chatbots first appeared near the end of 2022 as an experimental concept. Generative Artificial Intelligence (“AI”) and Agentic AI have received significant hype over the last several years. However, AI has not lived up to the hype for many companies.

Agentic AI is an offshoot of generative AI. Companies found that generative AI worked well at performing parlor tricks but was unable to solve their actual problems. The industry invented agentic AI to think autonomously and perform actions on behalf of humans. The goal was to automate complex tasks and make a company’s operations more efficient and less costly. The problem is that agentic AI works well in controlled simulations, solving theoretical problems, but not real-world ones.

When companies deploy various agentic AI solutions in live environments using real data to solve real-world issues, effectiveness and end-user satisfaction have fallen short—a reason some companies are abandoning AI projects.

Gartner published an article on June 25, 2025, titled “Gartner Predicts Over 40% of Agentic AI Projects Will Be Canceled by End of 2027,” which points out the issue: "Over 40% of agentic AI projects will be canceled by the end of 2027, due to escalating costs, unclear business value, or inadequate risk controls."

One of UiPath's major advantages is that it is historically a Robotic Process Automation (”RPA”) company, which is a software that is excellent at performing high-volume, repetitive tasks. One of the big issues that pure agentic AI companies have quickly run into is that performing high-volume, repetitive tasks is not in an AI agent's wheelhouse or core competency. Agentic AI works best with tasks that require some reasoning and choosing options, where the answer doesn’t fit in one neat box.

UiPath’s solution was to combine agentic AI (the reasoning brain) with the brawn of RPA (to provide the heavy lifting on repetitive tasks). So, although you might initially think that companies specializing in agentic AI might have an advantage over UiPath. It may actually be the other way around, and solutions from companies with pure agentic AI solutions may be at a disadvantage. The company’s excellent recent performance may be due to companies recognizing that UiPath offers a superior solution to both newer AI companies and larger AI platforms.

However, the company's move into agentic AI has only occurred over the last several years, and up until recently, UiPath has not done well. In several of my past articles on the company, I noted its execution issues. I also said in my March 7 article, "The company has plenty of long-term upside if it can execute." The stock's rise is due to the market recognizing that the company is beginning to execute.

After founder Daniel Dines returned as CEO (Chief Executive Officer) in early June 2024, he implemented several plans that included a company-wide restructuring that laid off 10% of its workers, revised its sales and marketing strategy, and implemented a strategy to merge Robotic Process Automation (RPA) and agentic Artificial Intelligence ("AI") automation on one platform.

CEO Dines's plans have resulted in the company's second- and third-quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 "beat and raise" results, which helped convince the market that the issues that plagued it in calendar year 2024 have stabilized, and the worst may be over.

UiPath's third-quarter FY 2026 revenue increased by 15.9%, representing a re-acceleration from 5% year-over-year revenue growth in the fourth quarter of FY 2025—evidence that CEO Daniel Dines' plan to restore revenue growth is working. The company's third-quarter FY 2026 revenue of $411.1 million beat company projections for between $390 million and $395 million.

The company's third-quarter FY 2026 ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) increased by 11% to $1.782 billion, beating company projections of $1.771 billion to $1.776 billion.

The market considers UiPath's fourth-quarter revenue forecast as a full-year guidance raise. The company gave its original full-year FY 2026 revenue guidance of $1.571 billion to $1.576 billion in its second-quarter results. When UiPath gave the fourth quarter of its FY 2026 revenue guidance of between $462 million and $467 million in the third quarter, it implied a raised full FY 2026 revenue guidance of $1.592.1 billion to $1.597.1 billion.

It provided FY 2026 ARR guidance of $1.834 billion to $1.839 billion in the second quarter. The company increased its full-year FY 2026 ARR guidance from $1.844 billion to $1.849 billion in the third quarter.

Last, let's discuss profitability: despite the company's increased revenue, the market may be unwilling to award it a higher valuation until it can prove it can grow revenue without increasing costs. UiPath was founded in 2005 and held its Initial Public Offering ("IPO") on April 21, 2021. The company has been unprofitable throughout its public history until recently.

Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Daniel Dines said on the third quarter earnings call, "We are on track to be GAAP profitable for the full year 2026 for the first time." If the company follows through on that promise, it may remove a major element that has been holding back its valuation and stock price since its IPO.

Although UiPath has a solid balance sheet and positive free cash flow ("FCF"), the market didn't like the prospect of its long-term future being in question, since unprofitable companies could eventually run out of cash.

If it reports annual profitability when it announces FY 2026 earnings on March 11, 2026, investors will likely be much more confident in the company's long-term future, which could drive a higher stock valuation.

Aside from UiPath's improved fundamentals, a significant reason to invest in the company is its combination of RPA and agentic AI on a single platform to automate complex business processes, may be slightly ahead of competitors' solutions.

Combining agentic AI and RPA

Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") & Chief Operating Officer ("COO") Ashim Gupta said the following at Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference:

Now dissect what is a process. A process is a series of 20 steps. Within those 20 steps, maybe 30% of them or 40% of them can be done on rule-based, task-based deterministic type steps. Another 40% or 50% of them can be done, but they need reasoning power. It's probabilistic in what needs to happen.

By combining RPA and AI agents, UiPath can automate 70% to 90% of most business processes. The last 10% to 30% is human monitoring of the process. The following image shows UiPath's platform, which illustrates how it combines agents, people, and robots to automate processes across industries and departments.

The middle of the image contains the word "Orchestration," which refers to a cloud-based platform named by UiPath as Maestro that organizes work among AI agents, humans, and robots.

Maestro distinguishes UiPath from some pure agentic AI competitors. Agentic AI has several significant issues, including hallucinations and security concerns. Suppose you task an agentic AI with performing a financial task, such as hypothetically managing an investment portfolio, and the AI hallucinates a solution based on how it believes the stock market works; it could mismanage the portfolio, leading to disastrous financial results. The worst part is that pure agentic AI can operate as a black box, making it hard for the user to determine exactly why the AI produced such poor results. Orchestration acts like the guardrails against a wayward AI agent.

The orchestrator may assign a robot to re-examine the AI agent’s assumptions, and if the RPA software finds that the AI agent violated a stock market “rule,” it may refer the decision-making process to a human. The possible mistakes that an AI agent might make are why it is essential to keep humans in the loop.

So how does the orchestrator know which rules the AI agent needs to comply with? UiPath has a tool called DMN (Decision Model and Notation) that allows users to convert complex rules into a decision table for either the orchestrator or the RPA to follow, rather than only relying on an AI agent’s decision, which may be based on out-of-date or biased training data. As a result, the orchestrator and RPA software govern when the AI agent can act and ensure its decisions remain within the bounds of defined rules. UiPath is among the first companies in the market to think along these lines, as shown by its introduction of Maestro, which it billed as the "industry's first agentic orchestration platform."

CFO and COO Ashim Gupta said at the Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference:

Process orchestration means now you have a 15 -- a 20-step process. Realistically, it's a 200-step process. You have humans, robots and agents working together, how are you going to monitor it? How are you going to govern that? How are you going to visualize it? That is what Maestro is, a product that we GA-ed earlier this year that allows companies to do that.

The above Ashim Gupta description is the simplistic explanation of what Maestro does. The following image shows some of the "in the weeds" features of Maestro:

When UiPath could only provide companies with RPA, or software robots, to complete 30% to 40% of a task, it was not worth automating specific processes for some companies. However, by automating 70% to 90% of processes, many more customers will be interested, thereby increasing UiPath's revenue-generating opportunities.

Risks

The ultimate promise of agentic AI is that UiPath sells AI agents for a premium. The risk for investors is that the "premium-pricing" future may take longer than expected to materialize.

Although agentic AI sounds exciting, the technology may not produce meaningful revenue growth immediately. Wolfe Research analyst Arsenije Matovic pressed management during the third-quarter FY 2026 Q&A, asking when the company would start seeing revenue from agentic AI and Maestro. Specifically, Matovic asked:

You doubled the number of customers developing agents quarter-over-quarter and Maestro process instances. What's the uplift you've seen in those customers? And is it kind of fair to assume that into next year as that revenue ramps, it can help sustain that NRR [Net Revenue Retention] and offset some of that low-end weakness that we've seen?

CFO and COO Gupta gave the following response:

We will continue to contend, but we're in the early innings of agentic...In terms of direct scalable, direct monetization place from agentic. We're -- at this we'll update everybody as we kind of get into next year around assumptions there. But for the immediate short term, there's nothing material as we cited in the script.

Currently, the company expects only indirect benefits from Agentic AI, in the form of selling more RPA and Orchestration, rather than benefiting directly from premium agentic AI sales. If the company fails to generate sufficient indirect revenue while waiting for the agentic AI market to become ready for prime time (meaning it becomes directly monetizable), the market may become impatient with the lack of increased revenue despite the AI hype and sell the stock off.

Although AI agents sound exciting, UiPath and others could find it hard to turn the technology into a profitable business because when UiPath builds an AI agent for a company, it is a bespoke solution. The problem with bespoke solutions is that the solutions are hard to replicate across the market. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Daniel Dines said on the third quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

We have deployed our teams in various use cases across various industries. We see some particular partners emerging in health care like, for instance, in revenue cycle management, prior authorization, claims management, in financial services, financial crimes, it's -- but again, I think it's a bit too early to mention one particular use case where we see like great replicable potential.

That statement means that, as of today, AI agents don't have a standard use case, and UIPath cannot use predictable, repeatable processes across its customer base to solve a problem. Instead, the company must create client-specific solutions, which makes the solutions non-scalable.

The problem is that software/cloud is highly profitable only when solutions have scalability, meaning the software/cloud architect (UiPath) can build a solution (AI agent) for a use case and sell it many times to other customers with similar problems or sell it across other use cases. Up until now, every agentic AI implementation has required engineers and support personnel for that specific customer, incurring a human-intensive cost higher than the cost of pure software or cloud services. Until management proves that "agentic AI has replicable use cases," the margins for providing AI agents will likely be lower than those of RPA robots.

The last risk that I will discuss is the competitive environment in the enterprise automation industry. Although several open-source RPA programs, such as Robot Framework, and open-source agentic platforms, such as LangChain or LlamaIndex, are technically competitors, UiPath's biggest competitive factors are companies with greater name recognition and platforms with greater scale. Examples of such companies include hyperscalers Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN), as well as platform providers Salesforce (CRM) and ServiceNow (NOW).

Although UiPath may technically have a better agentic AI framework compared to companies like Microsoft and Salesforce and may have a first-mover advantage in combining RPA, agentic AI, and orchestration, these companies are not standing still. Microsoft, in particular, has invested heavily in RPA over the last several years, and both Salesforce and Microsoft have invested in orchestration. If these larger competitors quickly catch up, it may stunt UiPath’s revenue growth and profitability.

Some of these larger companies can offer existing customers a bundled solution that essentially makes AI agents and RPA solutions free, which may outweigh UiPath's technical superiority in RPA and agentic AI, especially if customers believe the hyperscalers' AI agents and robots are "just good enough." If the collective competition from the big boys reduces UiPath's revenue growth or margins below investor expectations, the stock may lose its market-beating potential.

Valuation

UiPath has a trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) of 5.934, well above its March 7, 2025, P/S ratio of 4.777, but almost equal to its three-year median of 5.937, a sign of a fair valuation.

The following table compares UiPath's forward P/S ratio to estimated revenue growth and profitability with those of other companies it competes against in RPA and agentic AI.
